student_query: Does more tannin mean better wine?

matched_misconception.misconception: Tannin is the same as bitterness.
matched_misconception.why_incorrect: Tannin produces astringency — a tactile, drying sensation caused by tannin molecules binding to salivary proteins and precipitating them. This is perceived as a dry, gripping feeling on the gums, inner cheeks, and palate. Bitterness is a taste (not a tactile sensation) perceived on the palate and is associated with different compounds (e.g., certain phenolics, but not the same as astringent tannins). A wine can be bitter without being astringent, and vice versa. On the SAT, tannin is assessed separately from bitterness.
matched_misconception.corrected_understanding: Tannin produces astringency (a drying, gripping tactile sensation), not bitterness (a taste). Both can be present in the same wine but they are assessed separately and have different causes.
detection_signals: this wine is bitter because of the tannins
tannins are what make wine bitter
